answers.question: List of scenarios
answers.evidence: We analysed three different scenarios: 1. a deep decarbonisation scenario, which assumed the conditions under SSP1-1.9 and RCP 2.6 (average warming of 1.4 degrees C by 2100); 2. a status quo scenario, which assumed the conditions under SSP2-4.5 and RCP 4.5 (average warming of 2.7 degrees C by 2100); and 3. a carbon intensive scenario, which assumed the conditions under SSP5-8.5 and RCP 8.5 (average warming of 4.4 degrees by 2100).

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes by 2100 are described: 1.4°C (deep decarbonisation / SSP1-1.9 / RCP 2.6), 2.7°C (status quo / SSP2-4.5 / RCP 4.5), and 4.4°C (carbon intensive / SSP5-8.5 / RCP 8.5). No temperature overshoot is mentioned.
answers.evidence: a deep decarbonisation scenario, which assumed the conditions under SSP1-1.9 and RCP 2.6 (average warming of 1.4 degrees C by 2100); a status quo scenario, which assumed the conditions under SSP2-4.5 and RCP 4.5 (average warming of 2.7 degrees C by 2100); and a carbon intensive scenario, which assumed the conditions under SSP5-8.5 and RCP 8.5 (average warming of 4.4 degrees by 2100).
answers.notes: Temperature figures are described as "average warming" and sourced from IPCC 6th Assessment and NIWA. No overshoot language is used.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: The IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change) is named as the source of the RCP and SSP scenarios. External consultants were engaged to support the scenario analysis, but they are not named. NIWA is named as a source for temperature data. Other data sources named include NASA, Ministry for the Environment (MfE), International Energy Agency (IEA), Ministry of Transport (MOT), and McGuinness Institute.
answers.evidence: In developing the scenarios the representative concentration warming pathways ("RCPs") established by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change ("IPCC") 6th assessment and the Shared Socio-economic Pathways ("SSP") scenarios relevant for New Zealand were adapted to our industry and entity. [...] In May 2023, the CRD Working Group engaged external consultants to support our scenario analysis.
answers.notes: The external consultants who adapted the scenarios are not named. IPCC is the named provider of the underlying RCP/SSP frameworks.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: a deep decarbonisation scenario, which assumed the conditions under SSP1-1.9 and RCP 2.6 (average warming of 1.4 degrees C by 2100); a status quo scenario, which assumed the conditions under SSP2-4.5 and RCP 4.5 (average warming of 2.7 degrees C by 2100); and a carbon intensive scenario, which assumed the conditions under SSP5-8.5 and RCP 8.5 (average warming of 4.4 degrees by 2100).
answers.notes: The document explicitly lists both SSP identifiers (SSP1-1.9, SSP2-4.5, SSP5-8.5) and RCP identifiers (RCP 2.6, RCP 4.5, RCP 8.5) together for each scenario. SSP1-1.9 paired with RCP 2.6, SSP2-4.5 with RCP 4.5, and SSP5-8.5 with RCP 8.5 — these are from different IPCC assessment generations (AR5 RCPs and AR6 SSPs), constituting a cross-generation mix.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: In developing the scenarios the representative concentration warming pathways ("RCPs") established by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change ("IPCC") 6th assessment and the Shared Socio-economic Pathways ("SSP") scenarios relevant for New Zealand were adapted to our industry and entity. [...] The New Zealand reference data was overlaid to these scenarios, to develop detailed narratives and parameters to evaluate climate-related risks and opportunities.
answers.notes: The document explicitly states the scenarios were "adapted to our industry and entity" and that "New Zealand reference data was overlaid." This constitutes both localisation (NZ data) and industry/entity-level customisation.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: In May 2023, the CRD Working Group engaged external consultants to support our scenario analysis. [...] Members of the Executive Management Team as well as the CRD Working Group were involved in the scenario analysis and the development of management activities to address different risks and opportunities.
answers.location_hint: Scenario analysis section, p.31 and p.35
answers.notes: The scenario development involved internal staff (CRD Working Group, Executive Management Team) and external consultants, but there is no mention of participatory, co-created, deliberative, co-design, or broader stakeholder input processes.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.answer: No explicit standalone baseline or reference scenario is described against which the three climate scenarios are compared. The "status quo scenario" (SSP2-4.5 / RCP 4.5) could be interpreted as a middle-ground reference, but it is presented as one of three equal scenarios rather than a formal baseline.
answers.evidence: We analysed three different scenarios: 1. a deep decarbonisation scenario, which assumed the conditions under SSP1-1.9 and RCP 2.6 (average warming of 1.4 degrees C by 2100); 2. a status quo scenario, which assumed the conditions under SSP2-4.5 and RCP 4.5 (average warming of 2.7 degrees C by 2100); and 3. a carbon intensive scenario, which assumed the conditions under SSP5-8.5 and RCP 8.5 (average warming of 4.4 degrees by 2100).
answers.notes: The document does not explicitly designate any scenario as a "baseline" or "reference" against which others are compared. The status quo scenario describes "current policy, social, economic and technological trends continue" which is functionally similar to a baseline, but is not labelled as such.

answers.question: Model used
answers.answer: No specific climate models, IAMs, or macroeconomic models are named. The document references data sources (IPCC 6th Assessment, NIWA, NASA, MfE, IEA, MOT, McGuinness Institute) but does not name any specific models.
answers.evidence: The scenarios considered time horizons out to the end of this century (2100), supported by source data on the key trends over this period including: Temperature change (IPCC 6th Assessment report; NIWA); Flooding (Ministry for the Environment National Climate Risk Assessment 2020); Sea level rise (NASA); Population growth (Shared Socio-economic Pathways); Regulatory/Policy (Shared Socio-economic Pathways); Technology (International Energy Agency); Transportation (Ministry of Transport); Mitigation vs Adaptation efforts (McGuinness Institute).
answers.notes: Only data sources and frameworks are named, not specific climate or economic models (e.g., no CLIMADA, REMIND, GCM names, etc.).

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: Increased severity of extreme weather — Physical — RISK: Flooding of sites, health and safety risk, difficulty distributing content, business continuity risk — Expenses / Assets and liabilities / Revenue — High / High / Med — Short - Long [...] Extreme weather events - Auckland floods (Jan 2023) and Cyclone Gabrielle (Feb 2023) — Physical risk — Disruption to distribution of printed publications to affected regions; [...] Damage to company property disrupting services; Expenses — Low
answers.notes: The document includes both current (2023) damage from actual extreme weather events (Auckland floods, Cyclone Gabrielle) and anticipated future financial impacts from increased severity of extreme weather under the carbon intensive scenario. Financial impact levels are qualitatively assessed.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answers.evidence: The scenarios considered time horizons out to the end of this century (2100), supported by source data on the key trends over this period including: Temperature change (IPCC 6th Assessment report; NIWA); Flooding (Ministry for the Environment National Climate Risk Assessment 2020); Sea level rise (NASA); Population growth (Shared Socio-economic Pathways); Regulatory/Policy (Shared Socio-economic Pathways); Technology (International Energy Agency); Transportation (Ministry of Transport); Mitigation vs Adaptation efforts (McGuinness Institute).
answers.notes: These variables are explicitly listed as the key trend data used to build the scenario narratives. Each scenario parameter table (Figures 3.1–3.3) also includes NZ temperature change, sea level rise, flooding risk zones, population growth, regulatory/policy, technology, transportation, and mitigation vs adaptation efforts.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: In developing the scenarios the representative concentration warming pathways ("RCPs") established by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change ("IPCC") 6th assessment and the Shared Socio-economic Pathways ("SSP") scenarios relevant for New Zealand were adapted to our industry and entity. The New Zealand reference data was overlaid to these scenarios, to develop detailed narratives and parameters to evaluate climate-related risks and opportunities.
answers.location_hint: Scenario analysis section, p.31; Figures 3.1–3.3, pp.32–34
answers.notes: The scenarios are based on global IPCC RCP/SSP frameworks but have been localised to New Zealand using NZ-specific reference data (e.g., NZ temperature change from NIWA, NZ sea level rise from NASA, NZ flooding risk zones from MfE, NZ-specific regulatory/policy context). The scenario parameter tables show "NZ Temp change" figures, confirming regional downscaling.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Three time horizons are defined: Short term = next 1–3 years (aligned with business planning); Medium term = next 3–10 years (aligned with asset management and publication life); Long term = next 10–30 years (aligned with investor relations and radio stream life). The overall scenario horizon extends to 2100. Rationales are provided for each timeframe, aligned to specific business planning cycles and asset lifespans.
answers.evidence: SHORT TERM: Next 1–3 years — Aligned with business planning [...] MEDIUM TERM: Next 3–10 years — Aligned with asset management and publication life [...] LONG TERM: Next 10–30 years — Aligned with investor relations and radio stream life [...] While the time horizon for the scenarios stretched to the end of the century, we considered how the potential impacts would meaningfully play into the short, medium and long term timeframes for our business planning and capital deployment.
answers.notes: Justification is explicitly provided for each timeframe, linking them to specific business activities (business planning, asset management, publication life, investor relations, radio stream life). The overall scenario analysis extends to 2100.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: We evaluated the most ambitious and worst-case scenarios, to ensure that we stress tested all material risks or opportunities that might plausibly eventuate in the years to 2100. Taking this conservative approach also allows us to consider an environment where the physical impacts escalate much faster than anticipated, and/or the corresponding transitional impacts are put in place more rapidly, and to test our ability to respond.
answers.notes: The justification is that the three scenarios were chosen to span from the most ambitious (deep decarbonisation) to the worst-case (carbon intensive), enabling stress-testing of all material risks and opportunities. The document also notes that the IPCC RCPs and SSPs "relevant for New Zealand" were selected and adapted to the industry and entity context.
